input.counterSource: chrome.com/blog/masonry-syntax (Chrome's counterargument that grid approach requires more than one addition); css-tricks.com/masonry-layout-is-now-grid-lanes/ and webkit.org/blog/17660/introducing-css-grid-lanes/ (CSSWG Jan 2025 resolution adopting display: grid-lanes, a new display type); W3C CSS Grid Level 3 spec update March 2025 confirming display: grid-lanes as the final syntax.
input.evidence:
The claim has two components, both of which are problematic:

1. "Only one new value" — The WebKit article (Oct 2024) does use this phrase in advocacy for the "Just Use Grid" proposal, so the quote is accurately cited. However, Chrome's competing analysis (chrome.com/blog/masonry-syntax, updated Feb 2026) explicitly contradicts this framing: the grid approach also requires defining tracks on the non-masonry axis, potentially using grid-auto-flow for direction, and working around properties that become ignored — not a single-value addition in real use. The "one new value" framing is promotional language from a partisan source (WebKit/Apple favoring their proposal), not a neutral technical description.

2. "Without requiring a new display type" — This component is directly refuted by subsequent events. The CSSWG resolved the debate in January 2025 by choosing display: grid-lanes (a new display property value), confirmed in the W3C CSS Grid Level 3 spec update (March 2025) and the WebKit "Introducing CSS Grid Lanes" post. The final standardized form requires display: grid-lanes or display: inline-grid-lanes — a new display type, not merely a value added to grid-template-rows/columns.

3. Source quality issue: The primary source (webkit.org/blog/16026) is a partisan advocacy post written by the WebKit team while the standards debate was still open and unresolved. It is not a neutral specification document. Treating its advocacy framing as a settled technical fact is an overreach. The article explicitly solicited developer feedback to influence the outcome, which had not yet been decided at time of writing.
